claims.flip_evidence_text: any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement
claims.notes: Clause (ix) excludes only actions taken by the target at another party's written request; it does not carve out actions taken by Buyer itself. The answer 'No.' correctly reflects that Buyer actions are not categorically excluded from the MAE definition.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Material Adverse Effect” means, when used with respect to any Person, any fact, circumstance, occurrence, state of fact, effect, change, event or development that, individually or in the aggregate, materially adversely affects (a) the financial condition, business, assets, properties or results of operations of such Person and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (b) the ability of such Person and its Subsidiaries to consummate the Transactions before the End Date; provided, however, that no effect (by itself or when aggregated or taken together with any and all other effects) resulting from, arising out of, attributable to, or related to any of the following shall be deemed to be or constitute a “Material Adverse Effect,” and no effect (by itself or when aggregated or taken together with any and all other such effects) directly or indirectly resulting from, arising out of, attributable to, or related to any of the following shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or may, would or could occur: (i) general economic conditions (or changes in such conditions) or conditions in the global economy generally; (ii) conditions (or changes in such conditions) in the securities markets, credit markets, currency markets or other financial markets, including (A) changes in interest rates and changes in exchange rates for the currencies of any countries and (B) any suspension of trading in securities (whether equity, debt, derivative or hybrid securities) generally on any securities exchange or over-the-counter market; (iii) conditions (or changes in such conditions) in any industry or industries in which the Person operates (including changes in general market prices and regulatory changes affecting the industry); (iv) political conditions (or changes in such conditions) or acts of war, sabotage or terrorism (including any escalation or general worsening of any such acts of war, sabotage or terrorism); (v) earthquakes, hurricanes, tsunamis, tornadoes, floods, mudslides, wild fires, other natural disasters or other weather conditions; (vi) any pandemic (including the SARS-CoV-2 virus and COVID-19 disease), epidemic, plague or other outbreak of illness or public health event or any Law, directive, pronouncement or guideline issued by a Governmental Entity, including the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, or the World Health Organization or other industry group providing for business closures, changes to business operations, “sheltering-in-place” or other restrictions that relate to, or arise out of, any pandemic (including the SARS- CoV-2 virus and COVID-19 disease), epidemic, plague or other outbreak of illness or public health event or any change in such Law, directive, pronouncement or guideline or any interpretation thereof following the date of this Agreement or such Person’s or any of such Person’s Subsidiaries’ compliance therewith; (vii) changes in Law or other legal or regulatory conditions, or the interpretation thereof, or changes in GAAP or other accounting standards (or the interpretation thereof); (viii) the announcement of this Agreement or the pendency or consummation of the Transactions, (ix) any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement; (x) compliance with the terms of, or the taking of any action expressly required by, this Agreement; (xi) any changes in such Person’s stock price or the trading volume of such Person’s stock, or any failure by such Person to meet any analysts’ estimates or expectations of such Person’s revenue, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations for any period, or any failure by such Person or any of its Subsidiaries to meet any internal budgets, plans or forecasts of its revenues, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations (it being understood that the facts or occurrences giving rise to or contributing to such changes or failures may constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been or will be, a Material Adverse Effect); or (xii) any Proceedings made or brought by any of the current or former stockholders of such Person (on their own behalf or on behalf of such Person) against the Company, Parent, Merger Sub or any of their directors or officers, arising out of the Merger or in connection with any other transactions contemplated by this Agreement; except to the extent such effects resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to the matters described in the foregoing clauses (i) through (vii) disproportionately adversely affect such Person and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other Persons that conduct business in the regions in the world and in the industries in which such Person and its Subsidiaries conduct business (in which case, the incremental adverse effects (if any) shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or may, would or could occur solely to the extent they are disproportionate). (Pages 41-42)

Question:
Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)

Llama answer:
No.

Canonical claims:
- claim-17a61fc8626c: No.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. I need to evaluate a claim based on the contract excerpt, question, model answer, and fixed claim list.

 Let me parse the inputs:
 - Contract excerpt: Definition of "Material Adverse Effect" (MAE) with various carve-outs and exceptions.
 - Question: "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)"
 - Llama answer: "No."
 - Canonical claims: claim-17a61fc8626c: "No."

 Wait, the question is "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)". This seems like a yes/no question, but it's phrased oddly. Let me look at the contract excerpt to see what it's referring to.

 Looking at the MAE definition:
 - (ix) "any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement"
 - (x) "compliance with the terms of, or the taking of any action expressly required by, this Agreement"

 The question "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)" seems to be asking whether actions taken by Buyer are excluded from MAE, or whether the Llama answer "No" is correct.

 Actually, looking at the canonical claim: claim-17a61fc8626c: "No."

 The claim is simply "No." - which corresponds to the Llama's answer.

 Let me think about what the question is really asking. It seems to be asking whether actions taken by the buyer constitute a Material Adverse Effect, or perhaps whether the Llama's answer of "No" is correct.

 Wait, the prompt says: "For each claim return: correctness_label, load_bearing_label, flip_evidence_text, notes"

 The canonical claim is: claim-17a61fc8626c: No.

 I need to determine:
 1. Is the claim "No." correct based on the contract?
 2. Is it load-bearing?
 3. What would flip it?

 But what exactly is the question? "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)"

 This looks like it might be from a dataset where the question is asking whether a specific thing is true. Given the Llama answer is "No." and the claim is "No.", I need to evaluate if "No." is the correct answer to the question "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)".

 Let me reconsider. The contract excerpt mentions in clause (ix): "any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement".

 The question might be: "Are actions taken by Buyer excluded from the MAE definition?" or "Do actions taken by Buyer constitute a Material Adverse Effect?"

 Actually, looking at the structure, this might be from the MAC (Material Adverse Change) clause dataset where questions are asked about whether certain scenarios are excluded from MAE.

 The question "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)" is likely asking: "Are actions taken by Buyer excluded from the MAE definition?" 

 Looking at clause (ix): "any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement" - this refers to actions taken at the request of another party.

 Clause (x): "compliance with the terms of, or the taking of any action expressly required by, this Agreement" - this is about actions required by the agreement.

 Neither specifically says "actions taken by Buyer" broadly. Actions taken by Buyer (unless at the written request of another party or expressly required by the agreement) would NOT be carved out from the MAE definition.

 So if the question is whether actions taken by Buyer are excluded from MAE (or don't count as MAE), the answer would be "No" - meaning they are NOT excluded (or they DO count, or the answer to the specific question is No).

 Wait, the question format is ambiguous. Let me look at it again: "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)"

 This could mean: "Are actions taken by Buyer carved out?" Answer: No.
 Or: "Can actions taken by Buyer be considered an MAE?" Answer: Yes? But the Llama says No.

 Let me look more carefully. In many M&A contracts, the MAE definition is from the perspective of the "Person" (the target company). The question might be asking whether actions taken by the Buyer are excluded from the MAE definition for the target.

 Clause (ix) carves out "actions taken or failure to take action... at the written request of another party to this Agreement." If Buyer is "another party", then actions taken by the target at Buyer's written request are carved out. But "actions taken by Buyer" themselves wouldn't be an MAE to the target's business - Buyer's own actions don't affect the target's business in the context of the target's MAE.

 Actually, wait. The MAE definition is "any fact, circumstance... that materially adversely affects... the financial condition, business... of such Person". "Such Person" is the target. Buyer's actions don't directly affect the target's financial condition in a way that would typically be considered an MAE to the target.

 But the question might be from a multiple choice or binary classification where:
 Q: Are actions taken by Buyer excluded from the MAE definition?
 A: No (meaning they are not specifically excluded as a category).

 Looking at clause (ix), it says "actions taken... at the written request of another party". This doesn't say "actions taken by another party". It says actions taken by the target (such Person) at the request of another party.

 So if the question is specifically "Are actions taken by Buyer excluded?", the answer is No - the contract doesn't exclude actions taken by Buyer as a category.

 Therefore, the claim "No." is correct.

 Now, is it load-bearing? 
 A claim is load-bearing if changing the evidence text would flip the correctness of the claim. The claim is "No." - meaning the answer to the question is No.

 What evidence supports this? The absence of a carve-out for "actions taken by Buyer" generally. The carve-out in (ix) is for "actions taken... at the written request of another party" - which implies actions taken by the target, not by the buyer.

 Actually, let me reconsider whether the claim is correct. The question is "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)". This is extremely terse. It might be asking whether clause (ix) applies to actions taken by Buyer. The answer would be No, because clause (ix) applies to actions taken by the target company at the request of another party.

 So "No." is correct.

 Is it load-bearing? The evidence that would flip it would be a clause that explicitly says "actions taken by Buyer are excluded" or something similar. Since no such clause exists, the claim relies on the absence of such text, or more specifically, relies on the text of (ix) which only covers actions taken at the request of another party (implying the target's actions).

 Actually, in these annotation tasks, "load-bearing" usually means that there's specific evidence text in the contract that supports the claim, and if that text were different, the claim would flip. For a negative claim like "No", it's often harder to identify load-bearing text, but the relevant text is clause (ix) which carves out actions taken at another party's request but NOT actions taken by Buyer generally.

 Wait, I need to be careful. The question might be interpreted as: "Does the MAE definition exclude actions taken by the Buyer?" And the answer is No. The evidence for this is that clause (ix) only excludes actions taken at the written request of another party (which would be the target acting, not the buyer acting).

 Let me check if there's any other clause. No, there isn't a specific carve-out for Buyer's actions.

 So:
 - correctness_label: true
 - load_bearing_label: yes (because clause (ix) is the load-bearing evidence - it shows what IS excluded, and by implication what is not)
 - flip_evidence_text: something like "any actions taken or failure to take action by any other party to this Agreement" or more specifically, if the clause included "actions taken by Buyer" as an exclusion.

 Wait, what exactly is the flip evidence? It should be the text that, if present or changed, would flip the claim. Since the claim is "No" (actions taken by Buyer are NOT excluded), the flip evidence would be text that explicitly excludes actions taken by Buyer. For example: "any actions taken or failure to take action by Buyer" or changing clause (ix) to cover actions by Buyer rather than just actions at Buyer's request.

 But typically in these tasks, the flip_evidence_text is a hypothetical modification or the specific text that constitutes the evidence. Let me think about the format.

 Actually, looking at similar tasks, flip_evidence_text often contains the specific contract language that supports the claim, and if you were to negate or remove it, the claim would flip. But the instructions say "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". This suggests it's text that would flip the claim if it were different.

 For a "No" answer, the load-bearing evidence might be clause (ix) itself. Clause (ix) says "any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement". This implies that only actions taken AT THE REQUEST of another party are excluded. Actions taken BY Buyer (not at the target's request, but by Buyer itself) are not excluded. So the presence and specific wording of (ix) supports the "No" answer.

 If clause (ix) were changed to "any actions taken or failure to take action by another party to this Agreement", then the answer would be "Yes". So the flip evidence text might be clause (ix) as written.

 Let me formulate:
 correctness_label: true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement
 notes: Clause (ix) excludes only actions taken at another party's request, not actions taken by Buyer itself.

 Wait, but is this fully correct? Let me reconsider the question. Could the question be asking whether the target's actions taken at Buyer's request are excluded? Then the answer would be Yes. But the claim says "No." 

 The question says "Actions taken by Buyer". This is different from "Actions taken by Seller/Target at Buyer's request". 

 If I interpret the question as: "Are actions taken by Buyer excluded from the MAE definition (i.e., do they not count as MAE)?" 
 - Buyer's own actions wouldn't typically be an MAE on the target anyway, because MAE is defined with respect to the target's financial condition. But the question seems to be about whether such actions are excluded from the definition.
 - The definition looks at effects on "such Person" (the target). Buyer's actions might affect the target (e.g., if Buyer is a competitor, or if Buyer refuses to close), but generally in the context of this MAE definition, we're looking at effects on the target's business.
 - Actually, clause (viii) excludes "the announcement of this Agreement or the pendency or consummation of the Transactions" - this is related to buyer-seller interaction.

 But "actions taken by Buyer" - if Buyer takes some action that hurts the target, is that excluded? Not specifically, unless it falls under one of the carve-outs.

 So "No" is correct - actions taken by Buyer are not categorically excluded.

 However, I want to double-check if the question could mean: "Are actions taken [by the target] at the written request of Buyer excluded?" In that case, the answer would be Yes, because clause (ix) covers actions taken at the written request of another party (Buyer would be another party). But the question specifically says "Actions taken by Buyer", not "Actions taken at the request of Buyer".

 Given the precision of legal language, "by Buyer" is different from "at Buyer's request". The claim is "No." which aligns with the literal reading.

 Let me also consider if the question might be incomplete or truncated. "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)" might mean "Are actions taken by Buyer excluded? (Y/N)" and the answer is No.

 Actually, looking at the Llama answer and canonical claim, both say "No." So I should evaluate whether "No." is true.

 I think the safest interpretation is:
 Question: Are actions taken by Buyer carved out/excluded from the MAE definition?
 Answer: No.
 This is true because the MAE carve-outs do not include a general exclusion for actions taken by Buyer. Clause (ix) only covers actions taken by the target at another party's request.

 correctness_label: true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement
 notes: The carve-out in clause (ix) only applies to actions taken at another party's request, not actions taken by Buyer itself.

 Wait, is the flip_evidence_text supposed to be the text that would flip the claim if it were present? Or the text in the document that is the evidence? 

 The label is "flip_evidence_text". This suggests it's the text that, if flipped/modified, would change the answer. Or it could mean "the evidence text that supports the claim, which if absent/flipped would change the claim". 

 In many legal annotation tasks, "flip evidence" refers to the specific span of text that is load-bearing - if you changed that span, the claim would flip. So I should provide the actual text from the contract that is the evidence.

 I'll provide clause (ix) as the flip evidence, because if it said "by another party" instead of "at the written request of another party", the answer might flip to Yes.

 Actually, to be more precise about what would flip it: The flip evidence would be text explicitly excluding buyer actions. But since that text doesn't exist in the contract, the load-bearing evidence is the absence of such text and the presence of (ix) which narrowly defines the exclusion.

 Hmm, "load-bearing" typically requires specific text. For negative claims (No), it can be tricky. But clause (ix) is the most relevant text. The claim "No" is supported by the fact that (ix) only mentions "at the written request of another party" and not "by another party".

 Let me think about whether this is truly load-bearing. If clause (ix) were entirely absent from the contract, would the answer still be "No"? Yes, because there would still be no exclusion for actions taken by Buyer. So clause (ix) being present isn't strictly necessary for the claim to be true - the claim is true because of the absence of any clause excluding buyer actions.

 However, clause (ix) is the most relevant text that addresses the issue and shows that only a narrower category is excluded. In annotation schemes, this is often considered load-bearing because it's the evidence that distinguishes this case from a potentially different one.

 But wait - is there any reading where "No" is false? If the question is "Actions taken by Buyer-Answer (Y/N)" and this is from a standard dataset, maybe the expected answer is "Yes" because clause (ix) excludes actions taken at the written request of another party, and Buyer is another party? No, that would be "actions taken by Seller at Buyer's request", not "actions taken by Buyer".

 Let me reconsider whether the Llama answer of "No" is correct. In the context of the contract, if Buyer takes action against the target, could that be an MAE? The definition is about effects on the target. If Buyer is a party to the agreement, and Buyer takes actions that hurt the target, those effects are not carved out. So they could constitute an MAE. Thus, the answer to "Are actions taken by Buyer excluded from MAE?" is indeed No.

 I think my analysis holds. Let me format the output.

 One more thing: the claim is exactly "No." (with a period). The Llama answer is "No.". They match.

 Final check:
 - correctness_label: true (the answer "No" is correct)
 - load_bearing_label: yes (clause (ix) provides the relevant distinction)
 - flip_evidence_text: "any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement"
 - notes: Empty or brief note about clause (ix) not covering actions taken by Buyer itself.

 So I should return the evaluation for this claim.

 Structure:

   correctness_label: true
   load_bearing_label: yes
   flip_evidence_text: any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, at the written request of another party to this Agreement

 Or should I just provide the fields in a structured format? The prompt doesn't specify exact output format, but typically YAML or JSON or a structured text format is used. I'll use a clear structured format.

 Let me reconsider the load-bearing question one more time. Is the evidence truly load-bearing? 
 If the evidence text (ix) were changed to: "any actions taken or failure to take action by another party to this Agreement" - then the answer to "Are actions taken by Buyer excluded?" would be "Yes". So yes, this text is load-bearing. If it were worded differently, the claim flips.

 Actually, the text as written says "at the written request of another party", which means the target is taking the action. If it said "by another party", it would mean the other party (Buyer) is taking the action. So changing this text would flip the answer. Therefore it is load-bearing.

 I'll proceed with this.
